strings.explanation.form-i-pattern.fa3ala-yaf3al: Form I vowel class is faʿala–yafʿalu: past stem a, present stem a — like فَتَحَ / يَفْتَحُ. Common for transitive physical action. The vowel class must be memorised.
strings.explanation.form-i-pattern.fa3ala-yaf3il: Form I vowel class is faʿala–yafʿilu: past stem a, present stem i — like جَلَسَ / يَجْلِسُ. Common for sitting, dwelling, and intransitive motion. The vowel class must be memorised.
strings.explanation.form-i-pattern.fa3ala-yaf3ul: Form I vowel class is faʿala–yafʿulu: past stem a, present stem u — like كَتَبَ / يَكْتُبُ. The most common class. Roots with ح، ع، غ، خ often follow this pattern. The vowel class must be memorised for each verb.
strings.explanation.form-i-pattern.fa3ila-yaf3al: Form I vowel class is faʿila–yafʿalu: past stem i, present stem a — like فَرِحَ / يَفْرَحُ. Often stative or emotional verbs. The vowel class must be memorised.
strings.explanation.form-i-pattern.fa3ila-yaf3il: Form I vowel class is faʿila–yafʿilu: past stem i, present stem i. The vowel class must be memorised.
strings.explanation.form-i-pattern.fa3ila-yaf3ul: Form I vowel class is faʿila–yafʿulu: past stem i, present stem u. The vowel class must be memorised.
strings.explanation.form-i-pattern.fa3ula-yaf3al: Form I vowel class is faʿula–yafʿalu: past stem u, present stem a. The vowel class must be memorised.
strings.explanation.form-i-pattern.fa3ula-yaf3il: Form I vowel class is faʿula–yafʿilu: past stem u, present stem i. The vowel class must be memorised.
strings.explanation.form-i-pattern.fa3ula-yaf3ul: Form I vowel class is faʿula–yafʿulu: past stem u, present stem u — like كَرُمَ / يَكْرُمُ. Often expresses qualities or states. The vowel class must be memorised.
strings.explanation.form-root.assimilation-doubl: Form VIII with a doubled root: the Form VIII infix تَ assimilates with the geminate consonant.
strings.explanation.form-root.assimilation-waw: Form VIII with an assimilated root: the initial و of the root assimilates with the Form VIII infix تَ, producing a doubled letter (e.g., اِتَّصَلَ from و-ص-ل).
strings.explanation.form-root.assimilation-yaa: Form VIII with an assimilated root: the initial ي of the root assimilates with the Form VIII infix تَ, producing a doubled letter.
strings.explanation.form.1: Form I is the base triliteral pattern and carries the core dictionary meaning.
strings.explanation.form.10: Form X adds the prefix است and often means to seek, consider, or request the base meaning.
strings.explanation.form.2: Form II geminates the middle radical and often intensifies or causativizes the base action.
strings.explanation.form.3: Form III lengthens the vowel after the first radical and highlights acting with or toward another party.
strings.explanation.form.4: Form IV adds an initial hamza and tends to be a straightforward causative or declarative build on Form I.
strings.explanation.form.5: Form V adds the prefix ت and geminates the middle radical, often expressing reflexive or inchoative meaning.
strings.explanation.form.6: Form VI adds the prefix ت and a long vowel after the first radical, marking reciprocal or jointly directed action.
strings.explanation.form.7: Form VII adds the prefix ان and usually signals an inchoative or passive/reflexive sense of Form I.
strings.explanation.form.8: Form VIII inserts ت after the first radical for an internal or reflexive sense.
strings.explanation.form.9: Form IX adds an initial alif, geminates the final radical, and denotes colors or bodily defects, typically as stative adjectives.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.future.1p: The present indicative 1st person plural uses نَـ as prefix and ـُ as suffix; سَـ is prepended, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.future.1s: The present indicative 1st person singular uses أَـ as prefix and ـُ as suffix; سَـ is prepended, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.future.2d: The present indicative 2nd person dual uses تَـ as prefix and ـانِ as suffix; سَـ is prepended, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.future.2fp: The present indicative 2nd person feminine plural uses تَـ as prefix and ـْنَ as suffix; سَـ is prepended, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.future.2fs: The present indicative 2nd person feminine singular uses تَـ as prefix and ـِينَ as suffix; سَـ is prepended, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.future.2mp: The present indicative 2nd person masculine plural uses تَـ as prefix and ـُونَ as suffix; سَـ is prepended, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.future.2ms: The present indicative 2nd person masculine singular uses تَـ as prefix and ـُ as suffix; سَـ is prepended, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.future.3fd: The present indicative 3rd person feminine dual uses تَـ as prefix and ـانِ as suffix; سَـ is prepended, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.future.3fp: The present indicative 3rd person feminine plural uses يَـ as prefix and ـْنَ as suffix; سَـ is prepended, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.future.3fs: The present indicative 3rd person feminine singular uses تَـ as prefix and ـُ as suffix; سَـ is prepended, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.future.3md: The present indicative 3rd person masculine dual uses يَـ as prefix and ـانِ as suffix; سَـ is prepended, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.future.3mp: The present indicative 3rd person masculine plural uses يَـ as prefix and ـُونَ as suffix; سَـ is prepended, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.future.3ms: The present indicative 3rd person masculine singular uses يَـ as prefix and ـُ as suffix; سَـ is prepended, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.imperative.2d: The 2nd person dual imperative ends in ـَا (same as jussive — the ن drops), giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.imperative.2f: The 2nd person feminine plural imperative ends in ـْنَ, giving {arabic}.
The 2nd person feminine singular imperative ends in ـِي, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.imperative.2m: The 2nd person masculine plural imperative ends in ـُوا, giving {arabic}.
The 2nd person masculine singular imperative has no prefix, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.past.1p: The 1st person plural suffix is ـنَا, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.past.1s: The 1st person singular suffix is ـتُ, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.past.2d: The 2nd person dual suffix is ـتُمَا, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.past.2fp: The 2nd person feminine plural suffix is ـتُنَّ, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.past.2fs: The 2nd person feminine singular suffix is ـتِ (with kasra), giving {arabic}. Note: the masculine second-person singular suffix ـتَ has a fatḥa.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.past.2mp: The 2nd person masculine plural suffix is ـتُمْ, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.past.2ms: The 2nd person masculine singular suffix is ـتَ, giving {arabic}. Note: the feminine second-person singular suffix ـتِ has a kasra — they sound and look similar.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.past.3fd: The 3rd person feminine dual suffix is ـَتَا, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.past.3fp: The 3rd person feminine plural suffix is ـنَ, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.past.3fs: The 3rd person feminine singular suffix is ـتْ, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.past.3md: The 3rd person masculine dual suffix is ـَا, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.past.3mp: The 3rd person masculine plural suffix is ـوا, giving {arabic}.
strings.explanation.pronoun.active.past.3ms: The 3rd person masculine singular has no suffix — this is the base (dictionary) form, giving {arabic}.

The 3rd person masculine plural prefix is يَـ and suffix is ـُونَ, giving {arabic}. Note: compare with the second-person masculine plural, which uses the تَـ prefix.

strings.explanation.pronoun.active.present.jussi: The 1st person plural prefix is نَـ and the final short vowel is dropped, giving {arabic}.
The 1st person singular prefix is أَـ and the final short vowel is dropped (sukūn), giving {arabic}.
In the jussive, the dual suffix loses its ن and becomes ـَا, giving {arabic}.

The 2nd person feminine singular prefix is تَـ and suffix is ـِي (the ن drops), giving {arabic}.

strings.explanation.root.assimilated: Assimilated root — the first consonant is و, which drops in certain forms.
strings.explanation.root.defective-waw: Defective root — the final radical is و, which behaves irregularly and sometimes disappears.
strings.explanation.root.defective-yaa: Defective root — the final radical is ي, which behaves irregularly and sometimes disappears.
strings.explanation.root.doubled: Doubled root — the second and third consonants are the same letter. These often contract and geminate.
strings.explanation.root.doubly-weak-waw: Doubly-weak root — two of the three radicals are weak letters; the dominant one is و.
strings.explanation.root.doubly-weak-yaa: Doubly-weak root — two of the three radicals are weak letters; the dominant one is ي.
strings.explanation.root.hamzated-defective-waw: Hamzated-defective root — contains a hamza and a defective final radical و.
strings.explanation.root.hamzated-defective-yaa: Hamzated-defective root — contains a hamza and a defective final radical ي.
strings.explanation.root.hamzated-doubled: Hamzated-doubled root — contains a hamza and the second and third consonants are the same letter.
strings.explanation.root.hamzated-hollow-defecti: Doubly-weak hamzated root — contains a hamza, a hollow middle, and a defective final radical.
strings.explanation.root.hamzated-hollow-waw: Hamzated-hollow root — contains a hamza and a hollow middle radical و.
strings.explanation.root.hamzated-hollow-yaa: Hamzated-hollow root — contains a hamza and a hollow middle radical ي.
strings.explanation.root.hamzated: Hamzated root — one of the consonants is a hamza (ء). The way it is written changes depending on the surrounding vowels.
strings.explanation.root.hollow-waw: Hollow root — the middle radical is و, which usually shows up as a long vowel rather than a consonant.
strings.explanation.root.hollow-yaa: Hollow root — the middle radical is ي, which usually shows up as a long vowel rather than a consonant.
strings.explanation.root.sound: Sound root — all three consonants {root} stay the same in every form.
strings.explanation.tense-root.final-drops: In the jussive and imperative, the final weak letter drops completely: {arabic}. The remaining consonant takes sukūn.
strings.explanation.tense-root.final-isolated: When no suffix starting with ت or ن follows, the final weak letter is absent and the ending is a long ā sound: {arabic}.
strings.explanation.tense-root.final-lengthens-i: The final ي surfaces as a long ī: {arabic}.
strings.explanation.tense-root.final-lengthens-u: The final و surfaces as a long ū: {arabic}.
strings.explanation.tense-root.final-passive: In the passive, defective roots show a long ā ending at the final position: {arabic}.
strings.explanation.tense-root.final-resurfaces: Before a suffix starting with ت or ن, the final weak letter comes back as a consonant: {arabic}.
strings.explanation.tense-root.geminate-contract: The doubled consonants of {root} contract and geminate: {arabic}. This is the standard contracted form.
strings.explanation.tense-root.geminate-jussive: In the jussive and imperative, the geminate may either stay contracted or expand (e.g. يَمُدَّ or يَمْدُدْ). Both forms are acceptable.
strings.explanation.tense-root.hamza-seat: The hamza's carrier letter (its seat) changes depending on the surrounding vowels — it can sit on ا، و، or ي, or appear without a seat (ء): {arabic}.
strings.explanation.tense-root.initial-drops: In Form I active present, the initial و drops: {arabic}. It reappears in all other forms and tenses.
strings.explanation.tense-root.middle-lengthens-: The middle radical و lengthens into a long ā: {arabic}. All waw-hollow roots show this in the active past.
The middle weak radical surfaces as a long ī: {arabic}. This applies throughout the paradigm for yaa-hollow roots.
The middle radical و surfaces as a long ū: {arabic}. All waw-hollow roots show this in the active present.
strings.explanation.tense-root.middle-passive-aa: In the passive present, the middle of hollow roots surfaces as a long ā: {arabic}. Both waw- and yaa-hollow roots converge on this form.
strings.explanation.tense-root.middle-passive-ii: In the passive past, the middle of hollow roots contracts to a long ī: {arabic}. Both waw- and yaa-hollow roots converge on this form.
strings.explanation.tense-root.middle-shortens: The long vowel at the middle shortens and the final consonant takes sukūn: {arabic}. This happens in the jussive and in the imperative, which is derived from it.
strings.explanation.tense.active.future: The future is formed by adding the prefix سَـ or سَوْفَ to the present indicative form.
strings.explanation.tense.active.imperative: The imperative is built from the jussive by dropping the 2nd-person prefix. A helping hamza is added at the start if that would leave two consonants with no vowel.
strings.explanation.tense.active.past.form-i: The base pattern for the third-person masculine singular is {basePattern} — no prefix, with a {pastVowel} on the second consonant.
strings.explanation.tense.active.past: The active past tense describes a completed action.
strings.explanation.tense.active.present.indicat: The present indicative describes an ongoing or habitual action.
strings.explanation.tense.active.present.jussive: The jussive is used after لَمْ (which negates past actions) and in certain conditional constructions. It is derived from the present indicative; weak roots undergo additional changes.
strings.explanation.tense.active.present.subjunc: The subjunctive is used after certain particles (أَنْ, لَنْ, etc.). It is derived from the present indicative.
strings.explanation.tense.passive.future: The passive future is formed with سَـ or سَوْفَ followed by the passive present indicative.
strings.explanation.tense.passive.past: The passive past uses a fixed vowel pattern: ḍamma on the first syllable, kasra before the final consonant.
strings.explanation.tense.passive.present.indica: It describes an ongoing or habitual action performed on the subject.
strings.explanation.tense.passive.present.jussiv: The jussive is used after لَمْ and in certain conditional constructions.
strings.explanation.tense.passive.present.subjun: The subjunctive is used after certain particles (أَنْ, لَنْ, etc.).
strings.explanation.voice.passive.future: As with all passive present-based forms, the prefix vowel is ḍamma and the vowel before the final consonant is fatḥa.
strings.explanation.voice.passive.past: This is the passive past. The vowel pattern shifts: ḍamma on the first syllable, kasra before the final consonant (e.g. كُتِبَ — it was written).
strings.explanation.voice.passive.present.indica: This is the passive present. The passive pattern shifts the prefix vowel to ḍamma and places fatḥa before the final consonant (e.g. يُكْتَبُ — it is written).
strings.explanation.voice.passive.present.jussiv: This is the passive present. The passive pattern shifts the prefix vowel to ḍamma and places fatḥa before the final consonant (e.g. يُكْتَبُ — it is written).
strings.explanation.voice.passive.present.subjun: This is the passive present. The passive pattern shifts the prefix vowel to ḍamma and places fatḥa before the final consonant (e.g. يُكْتَبُ — it is written).
strings.favourites.empty: Search the database or build a verb and add it to your favourites list.
strings.footer.feedback.body: Muṣarrif is maintained by volunteers. Help us correct mistakes or missing paradigms.
strings.footer.feedback.title: See something off?
strings.formInfo.form1.relationship: Other patterns reshape this base meaning (causative, reflexive, mutual, stative).
strings.formInfo.form10.relationship: It extends Form I or IV; many roots also appear in Form II for a direct causative.
strings.formInfo.form2.relationship: Form V is its reflexive or passive counterpart.
strings.formInfo.form3.relationship: Form VI is its reciprocal or reflexive partner.
strings.formInfo.form4.relationship: Form X often expresses seeking or considering that causative meaning.
strings.formInfo.form5.relationship: It is commonly the reflexive or passive counterpart to Form II, covering gradual change or feigned behavior.
strings.formInfo.form6.relationship: It mirrors Form III with a stronger sense of mutual participation.
strings.formInfo.form7.relationship: Think "to become" or "to be acted upon" compared with the bare Form I stem.
strings.formInfo.form8.relationship: Compare with Form I; some roots also use Form VII when the sense is more passive.
strings.formInfo.form9.relationship: They rarely pair with other forms but still anchor to the base Form I meaning.
strings.formInfo.title: Form {form} insights
strings.languagePicker.label: Language
strings.mode.label: Mode
strings.rootInfo.assimilated.description: This is an assimilated root (initial weak), where the initial radical is a weak letter (و or ي). Assimilated roots often express actions of arrival, finding, or coming into being.
strings.rootInfo.defective-waw.description: This is a defective root where the final radical is specifically و. In conjugation, this final weak radical often drops or changes shape.
strings.rootInfo.defective-yaa.description: This is a defective root where the final radical is specifically ي. In conjugation, this final weak radical often drops or changes shape.
strings.rootInfo.defective.description: This is a defective root (final weak), where the final radical is a weak letter (و, ي, or ا). Defective roots commonly express actions that extend or complete, such as giving, building, or calling.
strings.rootInfo.doubled.description: This is a doubled root, where the second and third radicals are the same consonant. In conjugation, these two radicals often contract into a single geminated consonant.
strings.rootInfo.doubly-weak-waw.description: This is a doubly weak root with two weak radicals, where the dominant weak behavior is waw-like (و).
strings.rootInfo.doubly-weak-yaa.description: This is a doubly weak root with two weak radicals, where the dominant weak behavior is yaa-like (ي).
strings.rootInfo.doubly-weak.description: This is a doubly weak root with two weak radicals, often in the middle and final positions. These roots are relatively rare and tend to combine hollow and defective behavior.
strings.rootInfo.hamzated-defective-waw.descript: This root combines a hamza (ء) with a defective final radical that is specifically و.
strings.rootInfo.hamzated-defective-yaa.descript: This root combines a hamza (ء) with a defective final radical that is specifically ي.
strings.rootInfo.hamzated-defective.description: This root combines a hamza (ء) with a defective pattern (final weak). The initial radical is a hamza and the final radical is a weak letter (و, ي, or ا).
strings.rootInfo.hamzated-doubled.description: This root combines a hamza (ء) with a doubled pattern. It contains a hamza and has identical second and third radicals, which often surface as a geminated consonant.
strings.rootInfo.hamzated-hollow-defective.descr: This root combines multiple irregularities: it contains a hamza (ء) at the initial position, has a weak middle radical (و or ي), and has a weak final radical (و, ي, or ا). This combination requires special handling in conjugation.
strings.rootInfo.hamzated-hollow-waw.description: This root combines a hamza (ء) with a hollow middle radical that is specifically و.
strings.rootInfo.hamzated-hollow-yaa.description: This root combines a hamza (ء) with a hollow middle radical that is specifically ي.
strings.rootInfo.hamzated-hollow.description: This root combines a hamza (ء) with a hollow pattern (middle weak). The initial radical is a hamza and the middle radical is a weak letter (و or ي).
strings.rootInfo.hamzated.description: This root contains a hamza (ء). Hamzated roots can appear in various positions and often express actions related to eating, taking, or other fundamental concepts.
strings.rootInfo.hollow-waw.description: This is a hollow root where the middle radical is specifically و. In many forms, this consonant surfaces as a long vowel rather than a full consonant.
strings.rootInfo.hollow-yaa.description: This is a hollow root where the middle radical is specifically ي. In many forms, this consonant surfaces as a long vowel rather than a full consonant.
strings.rootInfo.hollow.description: This is a hollow root (middle weak), where the middle radical is a weak letter (و or ي). Hollow roots often express verbs involving movement, change, or speech, such as walking, rising, selling, or saying.
strings.rootInfo.sound.description: This is a strong root with all radicals being consonants. Strong roots form the foundation of Arabic verb morphology and can appear in all ten verb forms.
strings.rootInfo.title: Root insights

verbs.'wl-1: to lead to, to return to
verbs.byt-1: to become, to spend the night
verbs.Ddd-6: to be opposed to each other, to be contradictory
verbs.Ewz-1: to be in need, to lack
verbs.Hbb-2: to endear, to evoke love for
verbs.Hyy-2: to greet, to wish long life
verbs.jry-1: to run, to happen, to flow
verbs.jy'-4: to bring, to cause to come
verbs.jy'-6: to come to each other, to meet
verbs.mrD-10: to seek medical treatment, to consider ill
verbs.mrD-5: to feign illess, to become ill
verbs.msw-4: to enter the evening, to become
verbs.SbH-4: to become, to begin, to wake up
verbs.sff-4: to be excessive, to be vulgar
verbs.sEd-1: to go up, to be happy
verbs.SEd-1: to go up, to be happy
verbs.slm-6: to make peace with each other
verbs.zrq-1: to be blue, to become blue
verbs.rdd-6: to return or retreat little by little, to reverse
verbs.Eks-7: to be reflected, to be reversed
verbs.mss-6: to touch each other, to be in mutual contact
